sections.title: VFI

doc_references.note: Documented through the shared VPTO vector-thread fetch section.

sections.function_description:
Vector Fetch instruction. This instruction notifies the Vector Thread of the target address of a vector loop to be executed.

VPC = signed_extend(#vpc_offset * 4) + PC_of_main_scalar.
In the case of this instruction occupying multiple 4Byte encoding, PC_of_main_scalar always points to the PC address of the first 4 Byte.
sections.parameter_description:
#vpc_offset	Is the 16-bit signed immediate number to indicate the target address offset of current PC value of main scalar
#instr_num	is the 16-bit unsigned indicator of total number of instructions in this VF

sections.title: PUSH_PB

doc_references.note: Documented through the shared VPTO parameter-buffer section.

sections.function_description: This instruction pushes 256bit data of {Xt, Xm, Xn, Xd} wto parameter buffer of vector thread in one instruction.
sections.parameter_description:
Xd	Is the source scalar register.
Xn	Is the source scalar register.
Xm	Is the source scalar register.
Xt	Is the source scalar register.
sections.demonstration:
MOV       X0, #30
MOV       X1, #296
MOVK      X0, #32, #2
MOV       X2, #8192
MOVK      X1, #4096, #2
PUSH_PB   X0, X1, X2, X2
result:
	[63:48]	[47:32]	[31:16]	[15:0]
X0	0	32	0	30
X1	0	4096	0	296
X2	0	0	0	8192

Note X0[31:0] is used to incate which SREG would be initialized, and each bit corresponds to two SREGs.

In this example, X0[31:0] = 32’h0000001E, which means S9, S8, S7, S6, S5, S4, S3, S2 would be initialized, and others would be skipped.

Thus S2=32, S3=0, S4=296, S5=0, S6=4096, S7=0, S8=8192, S9=0.
sections.note:
Vector Loop Instructions
This chapter introduces the Vector thread mechanism and relative instructions, which is executed in main scalar and used to lauch corresponding function blocks to vector thread.

sections.title: VLOOPv2

doc_references.note: This ISA mnemonic has a standalone VPTO loop-control section.

sections.function_description:
VLOOPv2 instruction marks the beginning of one vector loop at a certain layer configured by #loop.
Loop count is indicated by Sn, which is an 16bit unsigned number. When Sn is 0, vector loop will not be executed.
#instr_num is a 16-bit immediate that refers to instructions number of its loop body, which declares #instr_num of instructions after this instruction belong to this vector loop. Please be noted that its loop body can also contain one or multiple VLOOPv2 at lower layers as long as their total instruction number won’t exceed VLOOPv2’s max capacity.
Sn used in the VLOOPv2 cannot be post-updated by other instructions in the vector thread (such as VLD/VST).
sections.parameter_description:
#instr_num	Is the instruction number of this vector hardware loop, not including itself. It cannot be 0.
Sn	Is loop count of this vector loop.
#layer	Is the 4bit loop layer indicator
4’b1000: this vector loop is in the forth inner layer in nested loop, and only has one  inner layer in this loop.
4’b0100: this vector loop is in the third inner layer in nested loop, and only has one inner layer in this loop.
4’b0010: this vector loop is in the second inner layer in nested loop, and only has one inner layer in this loop.
4’b0001: this vector loop is in the most inner layer in nested loop
4’b0000: If the layer does not meet the previous rules, it should be set as 4’b0000.
Note: if there is only one loop layer, #layer of vector loops in this layer must all be set to 4’b0001. If there are two loop layers, then #layer of vector loops in the most inner layer must be all be set to 4’b0001, and #layer of vector loops in the second inner layer must all be set to 4’b0010. More cases please see the Demostration. 
#last	Is the1 bit last for-loop indicator
1: this vector loop is the last loop in its corresponding loop layer.
0: this vector loop is not the last loop in its corresponding loop layer, i.e. there is still one or more cascaded vloops after this vector loop in its loop layer.
Note: If there is only one vector loop in its loop layer, #last of this vector loop must be set to 1
sections.note:
There must not be any SJUMP/SJUMPI/SCBZ/SCBZI /SEND instruction within a VLOOPv2
Sn must not be changed within VLOOPv2 that uses Sn as loop bound.
sections.raw_text:
VLOOPv2
Instruction Syntax
VLOOPv2 Sn, #instr_num, #layer, #last
Function Description
VLOOPv2 instruction marks the beginning of one vector loop at a certain layer configured by #loop.
Loop count is indicated by Sn, which is an 16bit unsigned number. When Sn is 0, vector loop will not be executed.
#instr_num is a 16-bit immediate that refers to instructions number of its loop body, which declares #instr_num of instructions after this instruction belong to this vector loop. Please be noted that its loop body can also contain one or multiple VLOOPv2 at lower layers as long as their total instruction number won’t exceed VLOOPv2’s max capacity.
Sn used in the VLOOPv2 cannot be post-updated by other instructions in the vector thread (such as VLD/VST). 
Parameter Description
#instr_num	Is the instruction number of this vector hardware loop, not including itself. It cannot be 0.
Sn	Is loop count of this vector loop.
#layer	Is the 4bit loop layer indicator
4’b1000: this vector loop is in the forth inner layer in nested loop, and only has one  inner layer in this loop.
4’b0100: this vector loop is in the third inner layer in nested loop, and only has one inner layer in this loop.
4’b0010: this vector loop is in the second inner layer in nested loop, and only has one inner layer in this loop.
4’b0001: this vector loop is in the most inner layer in nested loop
4’b0000: If the layer does not meet the previous rules, it should be set as 4’b0000.
Note: if there is only one loop layer, #layer of vector loops in this layer must all be set to 4’b0001. If there are two loop layers, then #layer of vector loops in the most inner layer must be all be set to 4’b0001, and #layer of vector loops in the second inner layer must all be set to 4’b0010. More cases please see the Demostration. 
#last	Is the1 bit last for-loop indicator
1: this vector loop is the last loop in its corresponding loop layer.
0: this vector loop is not the last loop in its corresponding loop layer, i.e. there is still one or more cascaded vloops after this vector loop in its loop layer.
Note: If there is only one vector loop in its loop layer, #last of this vector loop must be set to 1
Demonstration
	VLOOPv2, xxx, #layer=4’b0000
	              //not match, since more than one inner layer in it
	    VLOOPv2, xxx, #layer=4’b0001
	              //most inner layer, since no other inner layer in it
	    VLOOPv2, xxx, #layer=4’b0010
	              //second inner layer, since only one most inner layer in it
	       VLOOPv2, xxx, #layer=4’b0001         
	              //most inner layer
	    VLOOPv2, xxx, #layer=4’b0001    
	              //most inner layer, since no other inner layer in it
	  
	VLOOPv2, xxx, #layer=4’b1000                  // forth inner layer
	    VLOOPv2, xxx, #layer=4’b0100              // third inner layer
	        VLOOPv2, xxx, #layer=4’b0010          // second inner layer
	           VLOOPv2, xxx, #layer=4’b0001       // most inner layer

Note
There must not be any SJUMP/SJUMPI/SCBZ/SCBZI /SEND instruction within a VLOOPv2
Sn must not be changed within VLOOPv2 that uses Sn as loop bound.

sections.title: VAG

mnemonic_entries.syntax: VAG Ad, Sd, Sn, Sm, St

doc_references.note: This ISA mnemonic has a standalone VPTO address-generation section.

syntax: VAG Ad, Sd, Sn, Sm, St
sections.function_description:
Vector Address Generation Instruction.
Calculation of Ad varied with layers of nested loop:
One loop layer: Ad = const1*i1
Two loop layer: Ad = const1*i1 + const2*i2
Three loop layer: Ad = const1*i1 + const2*i2 + const3*i3
Four loop layer: Ad = const1*i1 + const2*i2 + const3*i3 + const4*i4
where const1 = Sd, const2 = Sn, const3 = Sm and const4 = St.
For example, at the end of each i4 loop, Ad = Ad + St. At the end of each i3 loop (when i4 reaching the ending loop count), Ad = Ad + Sm.
If multiple VAG instructions are aimed at the same Address Register (i.e., A0), the last one takes effect (supposing all previous settings are overwritten).
Address Register is used for Vector LD/ST instructions.
 

All the source Shared Registers can only be chosen from S0 to S31.
When Ad is used by post update instructions (such as VLDU/VSTU) in the loop, the source address must be consecutive at the loop boundary.
sections.parameter_description:
Ad	Is the destination Address Register.
Sd	Is the 32bit unsigned source Shared register for i1 loop, its ID must be even.
Sn	Is the 32bit unsigned source Shared register for i2 loop, its ID must be even.
Sm	Is the 32bit unsigned source Shared register for i3 loop, its ID must be even.
St	Is the 32bit unsigned source Shared register for i4 loop, its ID must be even.
sections.note: VAG must not be declared within a VLOOPv2

sections.title: VLD (deprecated)

doc_references.note: Documented through the shared VPTO aligned vector-load section.

vpto_references.note: VPTO documents the mnemonic through a family-level op rather than a distinct VISA encoding.

sections.function_description:
Vector aligned Load Instruction with offset from address register. 
This is a backward-compatible instruction of V210 architecture, hence DaVinci V300 deprecates the use of this instruction.
The source address is [Sn] + Am where Sn specifies the base address and Am specifies the offset. It load a vector data into the destination vector register Vd.

The offset in Address Register Am is automatically updated at each loop boundary (loop beginning).

The data distribution is the destination vector address in defined by parameter #dist. 
	
For #dist = {bdintlv, dintlv}, 2 destination register Vd and V[d+1] are needed, hence Vd must be even registers (V0, V2, …, V14). 
For #dist = {ds, bdintlv, dintlv}, 2*VL continuous data are loaded.

The actual accessing address [Sn] + Am should be 32B aligned by default. It differs a little bit for different #dist parameters as listed below.
Table 7 1 source address alignment for different distribution
#dist	Alignment (byte)	#dist	Alignment (byte)
normal	32	brc_b8	1

sections.raw_text:
VLD (deprecated)
Instruction Syntax
VLD Vd, [Sn], Am, #dist
Function Description
Vector aligned Load Instruction with offset from address register. 
This is a backward-compatible instruction of V210 architecture, hence DaVinci V300 deprecates the use of this instruction.
The source address is [Sn] + Am where Sn specifies the base address and Am specifies the offset. It load a vector data into the destination vector register Vd.

The offset in Address Register Am is automatically updated at each loop boundary (loop beginning).

The data distribution is the destination vector address in defined by parameter #dist. 
	
For #dist = {bdintlv, dintlv}, 2 destination register Vd and V[d+1] are needed, hence Vd must be even registers (V0, V2, …, V14). 
For #dist = {ds, bdintlv, dintlv}, 2*VL continuous data are loaded.

	5’h15, #splt4chn_b8: split one block into 4 interleaved 8bit channels.
	5’h16, #splt2chn_b8: split one block into 2 interleaved 8bit channels.
	5’h17, #splt2chn_b16: split one block into 2 interleaved 16bit channels.

#p	Is the post update enable bit
sections.demonstration:
For #dist = #splt4chn_b8, the load pattern interleaves every 4 8-bit elements together within each 32B block.
 
For #dist = #splt2chn_b16, the load pattern interleaves every 2 16-bit elements together within each 32B block.
 
For #dist = #splt2chn_b8, the load pattern interleaves every 2 8-bit elements together within each 32B block.
sections.note: #dist = #splt4chn_b8/#dist = #splt2chn_b8/#dist = #splt2chn_b16 is majorly for channel split before widening data type convert.
sections.raw_text:
VLDI
Instruction Syntax
VLDI Vd, [Sn], #offset, #dist, #p
Function Description
Vector aligned Load Instruction with immediate offset
The source address is [Sn] + (#offset*alignment_size) where Sn specifies the base address. It loads a vector data into the destination vector register Vd.
If #p=1 Source address will be Sn, and Sn will be updated to Sn + (#offset *alignment_size) after loading.
If #p=0 Source address will be Sn + #offset *(alignment_size), and Sn will remain the same after loading.
For #dist = {bdintlv, dintlv}, 2 destination register Vd and V[d+1] are needed, hence Vd must be even registers (V0, V2, …, V14). 
For #dist = {ds, bdintlv, dintlv}, 2*VL continuous data are loaded.

The actual accessing address [Sn] should be 32B aligned by default. It differs a little bit for different #dist parameters as listed below.
Table 7 1 source address alignment for different distribution
#dist	Alignment (byte)	#dist	Alignment (byte)
normal	32	brc_b8	1
brc_b16	2	brc_b32	4
rpt2_b8	2	rpt2_b16	4
us_b8	min(32, VL/2)	us_b16	min(32, VL/2)
ds_b8	32	ds_b16	32
bdintlv	32	dintlv_b8	32
dintlv_b16	32	unpk_b8	min(32, VL/2)
unpk_b16	min(32, VL/2)	brc_blk	32
e2b_b16	VL/16	e2b_b32	VL/8
unpk_b32	min(32, VL/2)	dintlv_b32	32
unpk4_b8	Min(32, VL/4)	splt4chn_b8	32
splt2chn_b8	32	splt2chn_b16	32


Parameter Description
Vd	Is the destination Vector Register name.
Sn	Is the source Shared register name, holding the base source address. 
Sn is a 32-bit unsigned address.
If #p=0, its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95
If #p=1, its ID must be any number between S64~S95
offset	Is the 8bit signed immediate offset in unit of alignment size
#dist	Is the 5-bit destination data distribution:
	5’h00, #normal: normal mode, load VL size data.
	5’h01, #brc_b8: load 1 data point and broadcast it to all elements, data type is b8. 
	5’h02, #brc_b16: load 1 data point and broadcast it to all elements, data type is b16. 
	5’h03, #brc_b32: load 1 data point and broadcast it to all elements, data type is b32. 
	5’h06, #us_b8: up-sampling by 2, load VL/2 data points, repeating each input element twice, data type is b8.
	5’h07, #us_b16: up-sampling by 2, load VL/2 data points, repeating each input element twice, data type is b16.
	5’h08, #ds_b8: down-sampling by 2, load 2*VL data points, reserve every other data points to Vd, data type is b8.
	5’h09, #ds_b16: down-sampling by 2, load 2*VL data points, reserve every other data points to Vd, data type is b16.
	5’h0a, #bdintlv: block-based interleaving load, load 2*VL data, even blocks are loaded into Vd, odd blocks are loaded into V[d+1] (A block is 32 bytes data).
	5’h0b, #dintlv_b8: element-based interleaving load, load 2*VL data, even elements are loaded into Vd, odd elements are loaded into V[d+1], data type is b8.
	5’h0c, #dintlv_b16: element-based interleaving load, load 2*VL data, even elements are loaded into Vd, odd elements are loaded into V[d+1], data type is b16.
	5’h0d, #unpk_b8: unsigned unpack, load VL/2 data points and unsigned-unpack to VL, write the result into Vd, data type is b8.
	5’h0e, #unpk_b16: unsigned unpack, load VL/2 data points and unsigned-unpack to VL, write the result into Vd, data type is b16.
	5’h0f, #brc_blk: load one block (32B) data points and broadcast to all blocks in Vd.
	5’h10, #e2b_b16: load VL/32 16-bit elements and broadcast each element into one block (32B). The source address is VL/16 byte aligned.
	5’h11, #e2b_b32: load VL/32 32-bit elements and broadcast each element into one block (32B). The source address is VL/8 byte aligned. 
	5’h12, #unpk_b32: unsigned unpack, load VL/2 data points and unsigned-unpack to VL, write the result into Vd, data type is b32.
	5’h13, #dintlv_b32: element-based interleaving load, load 2*VL data, even elements are loaded into Vd, odd elements are loaded into V[d+1], data type is b32
	5’h14, #unpk4_b8: unsigned unpack, load VL/4 data points and unsigned-unpack to VL, write the result into Vd, data type is b8.
	5’h15, #splt4chn_b8: split one block into 4 interleaved 8bit channels.
	5’h16, #splt2chn_b8: split one block into 2 interleaved 8bit channels.
	5’h17, #splt2chn_b16: split one block into 2 interleaved 16bit channels.

#p	Is the post update enable bit
Demonstration
For #dist = #splt4chn_b8, the load pattern interleaves every 4 8-bit elements together within each 32B block.
 
For #dist = #splt2chn_b16, the load pattern interleaves every 2 16-bit elements together within each 32B block.
 
For #dist = #splt2chn_b8, the load pattern interleaves every 2 8-bit elements together within each 32B block.
 

Note
#dist = #splt4chn_b8/#dist = #splt2chn_b8/#dist = #splt2chn_b16 is majorly for channel split before widening data type convert.

sections.function_description:
Initialize Load Alignment Register Instruction with offset from address register.
This is a backward-compatible instruction of V210 architecture, hence DaVinci V300 deprecates the use of this instruction.
Primed initialization of Load Alignment Register. VLDA actually loads a 32 bytes data to the Load Alignment Register from aligned address of [Sn] + Am.
This is the leading operation of a series of unaligned vector load/store, providing one-CPI throughput for every subsequent un-alignment load instruction.

The source address [Sn] + Am need not be aligned to 32 bytes, the hardware will truncate the address for alignment.
sections.parameter_description:
ULDd	Is the destination Load Alignment Register.
Sn	Is the source Shared Register holding base address for loading from the Unified Buffer. 
Sn is a 32-bit unsigned address, its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95
Am	Is the Address Register.

sections.title: VLDU(deprecated)

doc_references.note: Documented through the shared VPTO unaligned vector-load section.

vpto_references.note: VPTO documents the mnemonic through a family-level op rather than a distinct VISA encoding.

sections.function_description:
Un-alignment Load Instruction with offset from address register.
This is a backward-compatible instruction of V210 architecture, hence DaVinci V300 deprecates the use of this instruction.
This instruction loads data from address: aligned_tmp = ceiling((Sn + Am) / 32) * 32 into Vtmp, and concatenates part of ULDd and part of Vtmp into the destination vector Vd.
	Part of ULDd: from address (Sn + Am) to aligned_tmp.
	Part of Vtmp: from address aligned_tmp to aligned_tmp + (VL – (aligned_tmp – Sn - Am)). 
If the address (Sn + Am) itself is aligned to 32B, then Vd = Vtmp and no data part is from ULDd.
Alignment registers are updated by preceding VLDA or VLDU instructions.

After loading vector into Vd, ULDd and Am are post-updated according to St. If (Sn + Am + St) is larger than aligned_tmp, then ULDd is update by part of Vtmp according to the post-update size, otherwise ULDd remains its old value. And new Am = Am + St.

A VLDA instruction with the same Alignment Register as the destination must be executed before the corresponding VLDU instruction is issued in one single VLOOP.
.
sections.parameter_description:
Vd	Is destination Vector Register.
ULDd	Is source Alignment Register.
Sn	Is the source Shared Register holding base address for loading from the Unified Buffer. 
its ID must be any number between S64~S95
Am	Is the Address Register.
St	Is the source Shared Register holding the post increment value in unit of byte. 
St is a 32-bit unsigned value, its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95
sections.demonstration:
Supposing VL = 128 and the programmer need to load a vector at address 48.
 
Figure 7 1 Un-aligned Load (a)

Firstly, the programmer need to load the Alignment Register Ud through ‘VLDA ULDd, [Sn], Am’ instruction from the aligned address at 32. 
 
Figure 7 2 Un-aligned Load (b)

Then, a ‘VLDU Vd, [Sn], Am, ULDd, St’ instruction with the unaligned address 48 is executed and it will actually load data from aligned address 64 to 64 + VL = 192. 
 
Figure 7 3 Un-aligned Load (c)

Afterwards, the hardware uses part of the Alignment Register and part of newly loaded vector to form the unaligned vector, and write the result into the destination vector register.

  Figure 7 4 Un-aligned Load (d)

Then the Address Register Am is updated by St, and if the newer address is less than 64, then ULD register remain its old value, otherwise ULD is updated according to post-update size.
 
Figure 7 5 Un-aligned Load (e)

sections.title: VGATHERB

doc_references.note: This ISA mnemonic has a standalone VPTO block-gather section.

vpto_references.note: VPTO exposes a direct op-level correspondence for this VISA mnemonic.

sections.function_description:
Vector Block Gather Load Instruction.
This instruction gathers VL/32 32-Byte blocks to Vd.

Sn is the base address of all blocks and should be 32B aligned, each and each block’s address is calculated as:
block_addr[i] =index[i] + [Sn]. 
With 
index[i] = Vn[32*(i+1)-1:32*i]
And then it gathers VL/32 blocks of data (32 bytes) from each block address. Each index should be 32-byte aligned.
				Pg is the predicate on Vn and regarded as B32 format.
sections.parameter_description:
Vd	Is the destination Vector Register name.
Sn	Is the source Shared register name, holding the base source address. Sn is a 32-bit unsigned address, its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95.
Vn	Is the source vector register containing offset of each block. Offset is 32-bit unsigned and only VL/8-Byte of Vn is valid.

With 
index[i] = Vn[32*(i+1)-1:32*i]
And then it gathers VL/32 blocks of data (32 bytes) from each block address. Each index should be 32-byte aligned.
				Pg is the predicate on Vn and regarded as B32 format.
Parameter Description
Vd	Is the destination Vector Register name.
Sn	Is the source Shared register name, holding the base source address. Sn is a 32-bit unsigned address, its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95.
Vn	Is the source vector register containing offset of each block. Offset is 32-bit unsigned and only VL/8-Byte of Vn is valid.

sections.function_description:
Vector Block Stride Load Instruction.
This is a backward-compatible instruction of memory-based vector architecture, hence DaVinci V300 deprecates the use of this instruction.
Sm is the stride configuration register. Sm[31:16] = block stride and Sm[15:0] = repeat stride.
This instruction will read VL_BLK 32Byte blocks with start address of each block is calculated as: 
If #p=1, Addr_of_BLOCK[i] = Sn+i*block_stride*32.
And after execution Sn will be updated by Sn + repeat_stride*32
If #p=0, Addr_of_BLOCK[i] = Sn+ repeat_stride*32 + i*block_stride*32 
And after execution Sn will remain the same.
Sn must be 32B aligned.
If 32-bit predicate of certain block includes at least one bit that is 1, the whole block will be loaded to Vd.
If 32-bit predicate of certain block is all 0s, read request of this block wouldn’t be issued to UB and corresponding block of Vd will be set to all 0s, with exceptions won’t be raised even if address of this block is overflowed.

Sn	Is the source Shared register name, holding the base source address.

If #p=1, its ID must be any number between S64~S95

Sm is a 32-bit unsigned address, its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95.
Pg	Is the Predicate Register name. the predication mode is zeroing
#p	Is the post update enable bit

sections.function_description:
Predicate Load Instruction with offset from address register.
This is a backward-compatible instruction of v210 vector architecture, hence DaVinci V300 deprecates the use of this instruction.
The source address is [Sn] + Am where Sn specifies the base address and Am specifies the offset. It loads a vector data into the destination Predicate Register.
The actual accessing address [Sn] + Am should be VL/8 byte aligned by default. It differs a little bit for different #dist parameters as listed below.
Table 7 3 source address alignment for different distribution
#dist	Alignment (byte)	#dist	Alignment (byte)
normal	VL/8 (16B for L210)	us	VL/16 (8B for L210)
ds	min(32, VL/4) (32B for L210)	NA	NA

The offset in Address Register Am is automatically updated at each loop boundary.
sections.parameter_description:
Vd	Is the destination Vector Register name.
Sn	Is the source Shared register name, holding the base source address. 
Sn is a 32-bit unsigned address, its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95
Am	Is the source Address Register name.
#dist	Is the destination data distribution:
	2’b00, #norm: normal mode, load VL/8 size of data.
	2’b01, #us: up-sample by 2, load VL/16 size of data, repeating each bit twice.
	2’b10, #ds: down-sampling by 2, load 2*VL/8 size of data, reserve every other bit.
	Others are reserved.

sections.note:
Store Instructions
In this chapter, several kinds of store instructions are introduced:
Vector Thread support:
	Aligned continuous store to the Unified Buffer.
	Unaligned continuous store to the Unified Buffer.
	Unaligned continuous store to the Unified Buffer using alignment registers.
	Byte-aligned Scatter store.
	Element interleave store.
	Register spilling store to Stack Pointer in the Execution stage.
	Aligned and unaligned predicate register storing.
	Predicate Register spilling store to Stack Pointer in the Execution stage.
The alignment boundary is 32 bytes. 

All store instructions in this chapter (except VSTSP and PSTSP) are issued in the Store stage while VSTSP and PSTSP instructions are issued in the Execution stage.

Please be note that all store instructions would be issued out of order. Thus for store instructions write the same UB address(including SCATTER instructions which contains the same destination address), programmer need to insert a MEM_BAR instruction between them.

Sn	Is the source Shared register name, holding the base source address.

If #p=0, its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95
If #p=1, its ID must be any number between S64~S95
Sm	Is the source shared Register name, holding the address offset in unit of Byte

#dist	Is the destination data distribution:
	2’b00, #norm: normal mode, load VL/8 size of data.
	2’b01, #us: up-sample by 2, load VL/16 size of data, repeating each bit twice.
	2’b10, #ds: down-sampling by 2, load 2*VL/8 size of data, reserve every other bit.

Demonstration

Store Instructions
In this chapter, several kinds of store instructions are introduced:
Vector Thread support:
	Aligned continuous store to the Unified Buffer.
	Unaligned continuous store to the Unified Buffer.
	Unaligned continuous store to the Unified Buffer using alignment registers.
	Byte-aligned Scatter store.
	Element interleave store.
	Register spilling store to Stack Pointer in the Execution stage.
	Aligned and unaligned predicate register storing.
	Predicate Register spilling store to Stack Pointer in the Execution stage.
The alignment boundary is 32 bytes. 

All store instructions in this chapter (except VSTSP and PSTSP) are issued in the Store stage while VSTSP and PSTSP instructions are issued in the Execution stage.

Please be note that all store instructions would be issued out of order. Thus for store instructions write the same UB address(including SCATTER instructions which contains the same destination address), programmer need to insert a MEM_BAR instruction between them.

sections.function_description:
Vector Aligned Store Instruction with offset from address register.
The destination address is [Sn] + Am where Sn specifies the base address and Am specifies the offset. It stores a vector data in Vd into the destination address.
This is a backward-compatible instruction of V210 vector architecture, hence DaVinci V300 deprecates the use of this instruction.

The offset in Address Register Am is automatically updated at each loop boundary.
For #dist = {intlv_b8, intlv_b16}, 2 source register Vd and V[d+1] are needed, hence Vd must be even registers (V0, V2, …, V14). 
The actual accessing address [Sn] + Am should be 32B aligned by default. It differs a little bit for different #dist parameters as listed below.

Table 8 1 destination address alignment for different distribution
#dist	Alignment (byte)	#dist	Alignment (byte)
normal_b8	32	norm_b16	32
normal_b32	32	1pt_b8	1
1pt_b16	2	1pt_b32	4
pk_b16	min(32, VL/2)	pk_b32	min(32, VL/2)
intlv_b8	32	intlv_b16	32
pk_b64	min(32, VL/2)	intlv_b32	32
pk4_b32	min(32, VL/4)	mrg4chn_b8	32
mrg2chn_b8	32	mrg2chn_b16	32
sections.parameter_description:
Vd	Is the source Vector Register name.
Sn	Is the source Shared register name. 
Sn is a 32-bit unsigned address, its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95
Am	Is the source Address Register name.
#dist	Is the 4-bit destination data distribution indicator:
	4’h0, # norm_b8: normal mode, data type is b8.
	4’h1, # norm_b16: normal mode, data type is b16.
	4’h2, #norm_b32: normal mode, data type is b32.
	4’h3, #1pt_b8: store only the 1st element of the destination vector register, predicate register is neglected here, data type is b8.
	4’h4, #1pt_b16: store only the 1st element of the destination vector register, predicate register is neglected here, data type is b16.
	4’h5, #1pt_b32: store only the 1st element of the destination vector register, predicate register is neglected here, data type is b32.
	4’h6, #pk_b16: pack the source vector register Vd, extract the lower half bits of all elements and only store the active elements, data type is b16.
	4’h7, #pk_b32: pack the source vector register Vd, extract the lower half bits of all elements and only store the active elements, data type is b32.
	4’h8, #intlv_b8: interleave 2 source register Vd and V[d+1], and store the result (size of 2*VL), predicate register is neglected here, data type is b8.
	4’h9, #intlv_b16: interleave 2 source register Vd and V[d+1], and store the result (size of 2*VL), predicate register is neglected here, data type is b16.
	4’ha, #pk_b64: pack the source vector register Vd, extract the lower half bits of all elements and only store the active elements, data type is b64. 
	4’hb, #intlv_b32: interleave 2 source register Vd and V[d+1], and store the result (size of 2*VL), predicate register is neglected here, data type is b32.
	4’hc, #pk4_b32: pack the source vector register Vd, extract the lower 8bits of all elements and only store the active elements, data type is b32.
	4’hd, #mrg4chn_b8: merge 4 interleaved 8bit channel within each 32B block, predicate register is interpreted for 32bit data and works on data after channel merge. 
	4’he, #mrg2chn_b8: merge 2 interleaved 8bit channel within each 32B block, predicate register is interpreted for 16bit data and works on data after channel merge. 
	4’hf, #mrg2chn_b16: merge 2 interleaved 16bit channel within each 32B block, predicate register is interpreted for 32bit data and works on data after channel merge. 
Pg	Is the Predicate Register name.
sections.demonstration:
For #dist = #mrg4chn_b8, the store pattern shuffles every 4 8-bit elements together within each 32B block.
 
For #dist = #mrg2chn_b16, the store pattern shuffles every 2 16-bit elements together within each 32B block.
 
For For #dist = #mrg2chn_b8, the store pattern shuffles every 2 8-bit elements together within each 32B block.
sections.note: #dist = #mrg2chn_b8/#mrg2chn_b16/mrg4chn_b8 is majorly for channel merge after narrower date type convert.

sections.function_description:
Store Alignment Register Instruction with offset from Address register. The destination address [Sn] + Am need not be aligned to 32 bytes, the hardware will truncate the address for alignment.

This is a backward-compatible instruction of V210 vector architecture, hence DaVinci V300 deprecates the use of this instruction.

Denote:
dst_addr = Sn + Am
aligned_addr = floor(dst_addr / 32) * 32.
Addr_tail = dst_addr – aligned_addr
If the flag bit of USTd is 1, hardware will keep track of how many bytes are valid in USTd according to previous unaligned store instructions, denoted by valid_data_size, and USTd[(addr_tail - 1): (addr_tail - valid_data_size)] will be stored to UB from (dst_addr – valid_data_size) to (dst_addr – 1)
Dst_addr of this instruction must be the same as the post-updated address of the last VSTU that uses the same USTd, otherwise the hardware behavior is undetermined. 
Finally, set the flag bit of USTd to 0.
sections.parameter_description:
USTd	Is the source Alignment Register.
Sn	Is the source Shared Register holding base address to store to the Unified Buffer. 
Sn is a 32-bit unsigned address, its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95
Am	Is the Address Register.

sections.function_description:
Un-alignment Store Instruction with offset from address register.
This is a backward-compatible instruction of V210 vector architecture, hence DaVinci V300 deprecates the use of this instruction.

This instruction store un-aligned address the value concatenated from Alignment Register USTd and source vector register Vd. The destination address is dst_addr = Sn + Am.

Denote 2 aligned addresses as aligned_addr_0 and align_addr_1, where
aligned_addr_0 = floor(dst_addr/ 32) * 32 and
aligned_addr_1 = ceiling(dst_addr/ 32) * 32.

Supposing VL = 128, and the programmer need to store the vector at address 48.
 
Figure 8 1unaligned store (a)

If the flag bit of USTd is 0, then this instruction will writes the elements from aligned_addr_0 (32B) to aligned_addr_0 + VL (160B) in Vd to the destination address (It is in fact an aligned store at aligned_addr_0 with the elements from aligned_addr_0 to dst_addr being predicated to be inactive). As shown in Figure 8 2.
Then USTd will buffer the unwritten data in Vd and set the flag bit to 1.
After storing, the Address Register Am is post-updated to Am + VL

 
Figure 8 2 unaligned store (b)

If the flag bit of USTd is 1, part of USTd and part of Vd are concatenated and then write to aligned_addr_0, as shown in Figure 8 3.
	Part of USTd: from aligned_addr_0 to dst_addr.
	Part of Vd: from dst_addr to aligned_addr_0 + VL.
Then Ud will buffer the unwritten data in Vd and set the flag bit to 1.
After storing, the Address Register Am is post-updated to Am + VL.
 
Figure 8 3 unaligned store (c)

At last, there are some remaining data hold in the Alignment Regsiter, and a VSTA instruction can be used to store the remaining data to UB.
 
Figure 8 4 unaligned store (d)

Am	Is the Address Register.
USTd	Is source Alignment Register.

Supposing VL = 128, and the programmer need to store the vector at address 48.
 
Figure 8 1unaligned store (a)

If the flag bit of USTd is 0, then this instruction will writes the elements from aligned_addr_0 (32B) to aligned_addr_0 + VL (160B) in Vd to the destination address (It is in fact an aligned store at aligned_addr_0 with the elements from aligned_addr_0 to dst_addr being predicated to be inactive). As shown in Figure 8 2.
Then USTd will buffer the unwritten data in Vd and set the flag bit to 1.
After storing, the Address Register Am is post-updated to Am + VL

 
Figure 8 2 unaligned store (b)

If the flag bit of USTd is 1, part of USTd and part of Vd are concatenated and then write to aligned_addr_0, as shown in Figure 8 3.
	Part of USTd: from aligned_addr_0 to dst_addr.
	Part of Vd: from dst_addr to aligned_addr_0 + VL.
Then Ud will buffer the unwritten data in Vd and set the flag bit to 1.
After storing, the Address Register Am is post-updated to Am + VL.
 
Figure 8 3 unaligned store (c)

At last, there are some remaining data hold in the Alignment Regsiter, and a VSTA instruction can be used to store the remaining data to UB.
 
Figure 8 4 unaligned store (d)

Parameter Description
Vd	Is source Vector Register.
Sn	Is the source Shared Register holding base address to store to the Unified Buffer. 
Sn is a 32-bit unsigned address, its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95
Am	Is the Address Register.
USTd	Is source Alignment Register.
Demonstration
2 examples below shows the procedure of un-alignment store.
The 1st example, every i4 iteration stores a vector with size of VL, the Address Register is updated by VL at the beginning of every i4 iteration (const4 = VL), as shown in the pseudo-C code below:
At the beginning of every i4 iteration, Address Register dst_addr_offset is re-computed (CONST4 = VL and note that although in VSTU instruction, dst_addr_offset will be post-updated by VL implicitly, at the beginning of i4 iteration, dst_addr_offset is re-computed and post-update information is omitted).
Then Vector Register V0 is initialized to all 0’s.
Afterwards, VSTU instruction is used to store un-alignment vector updating dst_addr_offset and u0.
Finally, a VSTA instruction writes out the remaining data in the Alignment Register with dst_addr_offset post-updated by VSTU instruction.
	//i1 and i2 iteration neglected here  
	__align u0 = 0; //VSU zeroing alignment register  
	for(int i3 = 0; i3 <= iternum3; i3++){  
	  __addr dst_addr_offset;  
	  for(int i4 = 0; i4 <= iternum4; i4++){  
	    /* dst_addr_offset is re-computed every i4 iteration,  
	    that post-updating offset is omitted */  
	    dst_addr_offset = i1*CONST1   
	                    + i2*CONST2  
	                    + i3*CONST3  
	                    + i4*VL; //CONST4 = VL  
	  
	    __vector v0 = 0;  
	  
	    /* VSTU store concatenation of u0 and v0, 
	    dst_addr_offset is also post-updated by VL implicitly */  
	    VSTU(v0, base_addr, dst_addr_offset, u0);  
	  }  
	  /* VSTA store alignment register out and  
	  flush the flag bit to 0, the dst_addr_offset is post-updated in VSTU */  
	  VSTA(base_addr, dst_addr_offset, u0);   
	}  

In the 2nd example, as shown in the pseudo-code below, each i4 iteration will store longer vector data with size of 3*VL using 3 VSTU instructions and 1 VSTA instruction.
	//i1, i2 and i3 iteration neglected here  
	__align u0 = 0; //VSU zeroing alignment register  
	for(int i4 = 0; i4 <= iternum4; i4++){  
	  /* dst_addr_offset is re-computed every i4 iteration,  
	  that post-updating offset is omitted */  
	  __addr dst_addr_offset = i4*CONST4;  
	  
	  __vector v0 = 0;  
	  __vector v1 = 1;  
	  __vector v2 = 2;  
	  
	  /* VSTU store concatenation of u0 and v0, 
	  dst_addr_offset is also post-updated by VL implicitly */  
	  VSTU(v0, base_addr, dst_addr_offset, u0); //dst_addr_offset += VL  
	  VSTU(v1, base_addr, dst_addr_offset, u0); //dst_addr_offset += VL  
	  VSTU(v2, base_addr, dst_addr_offset, u0); //dst_addr_offset += VL  
	  
	  /* VSTA store alignment register out and 
	  flush the flag bit to 0, the dst_addr_offset is post-updated in VSTU */
	  VSTA(base_addr, dst_addr_offset, u0);    
	}  

Note

sections.function_description:
Un-alignment Store Instruction with immediate offset.
Variable size of data Un-alignment Store Instruction. This instruction will only store the least significant (#offset)-byte of Vd. #offset is an unsigned 9bit immediate that indicates the actual size of data to store. 
#offset should not be larger than VL, otherwise hardware behavior is undefined.
This instruction store un-aligned address the value concatenated from Alignment Register USTd and source vector register Vd. The destination address is dst_addr = Sn and it will be post-updated to Sn+#offset afterwards.
Denote 3 aligned addresses as aligned_addr_0, aligned_addr_1 and aligned_addr_2, where

	length = #offset
	dst_addr = [Sn].
	dst_addr_tail = dst_addr + length.
	aligned_addr_0 = (dst_addr / 32) * 32.
	aligned_addr_1 = (dst_addr / 32 + 1) * 32.
	aligned_addr_2 = (dst_addr_tail/32)*32.

Supposing a vector is stored at an unaligned address.

If the flag bit of USTd is 0 and dst_addr_tail< aligned_addr_1, this instruction will behave with the rule listed:
	Vd[ (length – 1) : 0 ] will be flushed to USTd[ dst_addr_tail%32-1 : dst_addr%32 ].
	Flag bit of USTd will be set to 1.
	No data will be written to UB.
 

If the If the flag bit of USTd is 1 and dst_addr_tail< aligned_addr_1, this instruction will behave with the rule listed:
	Vd[ (length – 1) : 0 ] will be merged to USTd[ dst_addr_tail%32-1 : dst_addr%32 ]
	Flag bit of USTd will be set to 1.
	No data will be written to UB.
 

If the flag bit of USTd is 0 and dst_addr_tail>= aligned_addr_1, this instruction will behave with the rule listed:
	Vd[ (length – dst_addr_tail%32 - 1) : 0 ] will be written to UB start from dst_addr to aligned_addr_2-1.
	If dst_addr_tail > aligned_addr_2, Vd[(length – 1) : (length–dst_addr_tail%32)] will be flushed to USTd[dst_addr_tail%32 - 1 : 0], and flag bit of USTd will be set to 1.
	If dst_addr_tail = aligned_addr_2, then no data will be flushed to USTd, and flag bit of USTd will be set to 0.
 

If the flag bit of USTd is 1 and dst_addr_tail>= aligned_addr_1, this instruction will behave with the rule listed: 
	USTd[dst_addr%32-1 : (dst_addr%32-USTd_valid_length)] will be written to UB start from (dst_addr – USTd_valid_length) to (dst_addr – 1)
	Vd[ (length – dst_addr_tail%32 - 1) : 0 ] will be written to UB start from dst_addr to (aligned_addr_2-1).
	If dst_addr_tail > aligned_addr_2, Vd[(length – 1) : (length–dst_addr_tail%32)] will be flushed to USTd[dst_addr_tail%32 - 1 : 0], and flag bit of USTd will be set to 1.
	If dst_addr_tail = aligned_addr_2, then no data will be flushed to USTd, and flag bit of USTd will be set to 0.

sections.function_description:
Predicate Store Instruction with offset from address register.
This is a backward-compatible instruction of V210 vector architecture, hence DaVinci V300 deprecates the use of this instruction.

The destination address is [Sn] + Am where Sn specifies the base address and Am specifies the offset. It stores a predication vector into the destination address.

The actual accessing address [Sn] + Am should be VL/8 byte aligned by default. It differs a little bit for different #dist parameters as listed below.
Table 8 2 destination address alignment for different distribution
#dist	Alignment (byte)	#dist	Alignment (byte)
normal	VL/8 (16B for L210)	pk	VL/16 (8B for L210)

The offset in Address Register Am is automatically updated at each loop boundary.
The predication data type is always b8.
sections.parameter_description:
Vd	Is the destination Vector Register name.
Sn	Is the source Shared register name, holding the base source address. Sn is a 32-bit unsigned value, its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95
Am	Is the source Address Register name.
#dist	Is the 1-bit destination data distribution indicator:
	1’b0, #norm: normal mode.
	1’b1, #pk: pack the source vector register Pd, reserve every other bit.

sections.title: VSTUR

doc_references.note: This ISA mnemonic has a standalone VPTO register-update variable-size store section.

vpto_references.note: VPTO exposes a direct op-level correspondence for this VISA mnemonic.

sections.function_description:
Un-alignment store after element squeezing.
The actual store address is [Sn] + AR, it can be unaligned.
Denote 3 aligned addresses as aligned_addr_0, aligned_addr_1 and aligned_addr_2, where

	length = SQZN
	dst_addr = [Sn] + AR.
	dst_addr_tail = dst_addr + length.
	aligned_addr_0 = (dst_addr / 32) * 32.
	aligned_addr_1 = (dst_addr / 32 + 1) * 32.
	aligned_addr_2 = (dst_addr_tail/32)*32.

Supposing a vector is stored at an unaligned address.

If the flag bit of USTd is 0 and dst_addr_tail< aligned_addr_1, this instruction will behave with the rule listed:
	Vd[ (length – 1) : 0 ] will be flushed to USTd[ dst_addr_tail%32-1 : dst_addr%32 ].
	Flag bit of USTd will be set to 1.
	No data will be written to UB.
 

If the If the flag bit of USTd is 1 and dst_addr_tail< aligned_addr_1, this instruction will behave with the rule listed:
	Vd[ (length – 1) : 0 ] will be merged to USTd[ dst_addr_tail%32-1 : dst_addr%32 ]
	Flag bit of USTd will be set to 1.
	No data will be written to UB.
 

If the flag bit of USTd is 0 and dst_addr_tail>= aligned_addr_1, this instruction will behave with the rule listed:
	Vd[ (length – dst_addr_tail%32 - 1) : 0 ] will be written to UB start from dst_addr to (aligned_addr_2-1).
	If dst_addr_tail > aligned_addr_2, Vd[(length – 1) : (length–dst_addr_tail%32)] will be flushed to USTd[dst_addr_tail%32 - 1 : 0], and flag bit of USTd will be set to 1.
	If dst_addr_tail = aligned_addr_2, then no data will be flushed to USTd, and flag bit of USTd will be set to 0.
 

If the flag bit of USTd is 1 and dst_addr_tail>= aligned_addr_1, this instruction will behave with the rule listed: 
	USTd[dst_addr%32-1 : (dst_addr%32-USTd_valid_length)] will be written to UB start from (dst_addr – USTd_valid_length) to (dst_addr – 1)
	Vd[ (length – dst_addr_tail%32 - 1) : 0 ] will be written to UB start from dst_addr to aligned_addr_2-1.
	If dst_addr_tail > aligned_addr_2, Vd[(length – 1) : (length–dst_addr_tail%32)] will be flushed to USTd[dst_addr_tail%32 - 1 : 0], and flag bit of USTd will be set to 1.
	If dst_addr_tail = aligned_addr_2, then no data will be flushed to USTd, and flag bit of USTd will be set to 0.
 

Finally, SPR AR is updated by the value in SPR SQZN if #p=1. Otherwise AR remains its old value.
sections.parameter_description:
Vd	Is the source Vector Register name.
Sn	Is the source Shared Register name. 
Sn is a 32-bit unsigned address
its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95
USTd	Is the source and destination Alignment Register name.
SQZN	Is the SPR SQZN.
AR	Is the SPR AR.
#p	Is the post update enable bit for SPR.AR

sections.title: VSTAR

doc_references.note: This ISA mnemonic has a standalone VPTO register-update align flush section.

vpto_references.note: VPTO exposes a direct op-level correspondence for this VISA mnemonic.

sections.function_description:
Store Alignment Register Instruction.
Denote dst_addr = Sn +AR and aligned_addr = (dst_addr / 32) * 32.
Addr_tail = dst_addr – aligned_addr
If the flag bit of USTd is 1, hardware will keep track of how many bytes are valid in USTd according to previous unaligned store instructions, denoted by valid_data_size, and USTd[(addr_tail - 1): (addr_tail - valid_data_size)] will be stored to UB from (dst_addr – valid_data_size) to (dst_addr – 1)
Dst_addr of this instruction must be the same as the post-updated address of the last VSTUR that uses the same USTd, otherwise the hardware behavior is undetermined. 
Finally, set the flag bit of USTd to 0.
sections.parameter_description:
USTd	Is the source Alignment Register.
Sn	Is the source Shared Register holding base address to store to the Unified Buffer. 
Sn is a 32-bit unsigned address
its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95
AR	Is the SPR AR.

sections.title: SPRSTI

doc_references.note: Documented through the shared VPTO SPR store and clear section.

sections.function_description:
SPR Store Instruction with immediate offset.
Sn specifies the base address and #offset specifies the signed 8bit offset.
#p is the post-update enable control.
If #p=1, destination address will be Sn, and Sn will be updated to Sn+ (#offset*SPR_DATA_WIDTH) after storing.
If #p=0, destination address will be Sn+(#offset* SPR_DATA_WIDTH), and Sn will remain the same after loading.
The source address must be aligned to data width of corresponding SPR.
sections.parameter_description:
#SPR_ID	Is the 7bit ID of SPR, currently only #SPR_ID=7'b100_1010(AR) is allowed.
Sn	Is the source Shared register name, holding the base source address. 
If #p=0, its ID must be even in S0~S63, or any number between S64~S95
If #p=1, its ID must be any number between S64~S95
#offset	Is the 8bit signed immediate offset in unit of alignment size
#p	Is the post update enable bit

sections.title: VCADD

doc_references.note: This ISA mnemonic has a standalone VPTO reduction-add section.

vpto_references.note: VPTO exposes a direct op-level correspondence for this VISA mnemonic.

sections.function_description:
Vector Addition Reduction Instruction.
Add all active elements together and write the result to the lowest element of the destination vector register. Other element positions are filled with all 0s.
Inactive elements are treated as 0.

For u8 and s8 data type, the result is u16 and s16 respectively.
For u16 and s16 data type, the result is u32 and s32 respectively.
For u32 and s32 data type, the result is u32 and s32 respectively.
For f16 and f32 data type, the result is f16 and f32 respectively.
If Pg are all 0’s, 0(+0 for floating points) will be written to Vd.
sections.parameter_description:
.type	Is the element data type, it can be u8, s8, u16, s16, u32, s32, f16, f32.
	3’b000: u8.
	3’b001: s8.
	3’b010: u16.
	3’b011: s16.
	3’b100: u32.
	3’b101: s32.
	3’b110: f16.
	3’b111: f32.
Vd	Is the destination Vector Register name.
Vn	Is the source Vector Register name.
Pg	Is the Predicate Register name.
sections.note: By default, the results are not saturated, so the signed and unsigned data type (with the same data width) are implemented exactly the same.

sections.title: VCMAX

doc_references.note: This ISA mnemonic has a standalone VPTO reduction-max section.

vpto_references.note: VPTO exposes a direct op-level correspondence for this VISA mnemonic.

sections.function_description:
Vector Maximum Reduction Instruction.
Find the maximum element in the source vector and write the result to the lowest element of the destination vector register. 
Also index of the maximum element will be written to the second lowest element of the destination vector register. If there are multiple maximum elements, the minimum index will be written.
Other element positions are filled with all 0s.
For floating-point type, inactive elements are treated as –INF. If all elements are inactive, then –INF is written to the lowest element of Vd, and other element positions are filled with all 0s. Note that the –INF data caused by inactive elements will not raise exception. 
For integer type, inactive elements are treated as literal minimum value. If all elements are inactive, then this literal minimum value is written to the lowest element of Vd, and other element positions are filled with all 0s.
sections.parameter_description:
.type	Is the element data type, it can be u8, s8, u16, s16, u32, s32, f16, f32.
	3’b000: u8.
	3’b001: s8.
	3’b010: u16.
	3’b011: s16.
	3’b100: u32.
	3’b101: s32.
	3’b110: f16.
	3’b111: f32.
Vd	Is the destination Vector Register name.
Vn	Is the source Vector Register name.
Pg	Is the Predicate Register name.
sections.note:
max(+0, -0) = +0.
If the input contains NaN, the output element is NaN(before saturation), and the index is position of first NaN.

After all blocks’ results are written to LSBs of Vd, other element positions are filled with all 0s.

sections.title: VCGMIN

doc_references.note: Documented through the shared VPTO grouped-reduction section.

sections.function_description:
Vector Minimum Reduction Instruction.
Find the minimum element in each block of the source vector and write the result continuously to the LSBs of the destination vector register.
Other element positions are filled with all 0s.
For floating-point type, inactive elements are treated as +INF. If all elements in one block are inactive, then +INF is written to the corresponding element position of Vd. After all blocks’ results are written to LSBs of Vd, other element positions are filled with all 0s. Note that the +INF data caused by inactive elements will not raise exception.
For integer type, inactive elements are treated as literal maximum value. If all elements in one block are inactive, then this literal maximum value is written to the corresponding element position of Vd. After all blocks’ results are written to LSBs of Vd, other element positions are filled with all 0s.

	3’b110: f16.

sections.title: VSQZ

doc_references.note: Documented through the shared VPTO squeeze and unsqueeze section.

sections.function_description:
Squeeze the source vector by retaining active elements and discarding inactive elements from the source vector register and place the result into the destination vector register, the rest element positions are all zeros.
The byte size of remaining element after squeezing is written to the SPR SQZN.
#st is a hardware hint that indicates whether the result of VSQZ will be stored to unified buffer or not. 
If the result of VSQZ will be stored to unified buffer, then SQZN of current VSQZ will be pushed to VSTUR for further store operation, otherwise SQZN will be dropped.
Programmer must guarantee that total number of VSTUR is equal to the total number of VSQZ with #st=1
Also to avoid dead lock of hardware, a VSTUR must be inserted between two VSQZ with #st = 1
sections.parameter_description:
.type	Is the element data type, it can be b8, b16, b32.
	2’b00: b8.
	2’b01: b16.
	2’b10: b32.
	2’b11: reserved.
Vd	Is the destination Vector Register name.
Vn	Is the source Vector Register name.
Pg	Is the Predicate Register name.
SQZN	Is the SPR to store the byte size of remaining elements.
#st	Is the hardware hint
#0: result of VSQZ will not be stored.
#1: result of VSQZ will be stored.
sections.demonstration: For example, supposing there are only 8 b16 elements in each vector register (VL_16 = 8), the source vector register is (7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0), the destination vector register is (8, 8, 8, 8, 8, 8, 8, 8) and the source predicate vector is 8’b00101001, then the squeezed vector is (5, 3, 0), to fill the reset elements with VL_16 = 8, the destination vector should be (0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 5, 3, 0), and the SPR SQZN is 6 bytes, which implies a zeroing predication mode of Vd writing

Programmer must guarantee that total number of VSTUR is equal to the total number of VSQZ with #st=1
Also to avoid dead lock of hardware, a VSTUR must be inserted between two VSQZ with #st = 1

sections.raw_text:
VUSQZ
Instruction Syntax
VUSQZ.type Vd, Pg
Function Description
Unsqueeze vector elements according to the predicate register. Element i in the destination vector register equals to the count of 1 from element 0 to element (i – 1) in the governing predicate register. The most significant predicate element is ignored.
	element_ dst[VL_t]; //Vd  
	pred_ pg[VL_t]; //Pg  
	dst[0] = 0;  
	for(int i = 1; i < VL_t; i++){  
	  dst[i] = pg[i – 1] ? (dst[i – 1] + 1) : dst[i - 1];  
	}  

Parameter Description
.type	Is the element data type, it can be b8, b16, b32.
	2’b00: b8.
	2’b01: b16.
	2’b10: b32.
	2’b11: reserved.
Vd	Is the destination Vector Register name.
Pg	Is the Predicate Register name.
Demonstration
For example, supposing there are only 8 s16 elements in each vector register and the predicate register is 8’b0110_0100. Then the destination vector is 32111000 (from msb to lsb, each element is s16).
Note

sections.function_description:
Conversion from floating-point to integer.

For conversion from wider data type to narrower data type, the destination element is placed into the even part or odd part of each wider element position while the other part filled with all zeros, which implies a zeroing predication mode even if this instruction have no Pg for predication.
 
Figure 13 1 wider to narrower

For conversion from narrower data type to wider data type, only even or odd input elements are converted, the other part is unused and neglected.
 
Figure 13 2 narrower to wider

For saturation conversation, any converting result exceeding the maximum and minimum range will be saturated.
For VCVTFI.f162s4, every 2 s4 results are stored to Vd continuously.
 
Predication Pg is always applied to the input lanes, for wider, narrower or same-width conversion. 
For wider/narrower conversion, predication must be merged with #part/#pp indicator to determine which input/output lane is valid. Take conversion between 32bit and 16bit data type as example:
 
Figure 13 3 narrower to wider

  Figure 13 4 wider to narrower
Especially for f16 to s4 conversion, 2 continuous f16 elements are converted into 2 continuous s4 elements together. Hardware will apply predicate of even input lanes to the neighbor odd input lanes regardless of the predicate of odd lanes
sections.parameter_description:
Vd	Is the destination Vector Register name.
Vn	Is the source Vector Register name.
type	Is the 5-bit convert type indicator
type={fp_src_type, int_dst_type}
type[4:3] = fp_type:
	2’b00:f32
	2’b01:f16
	2’b10:bf16
type[2:0] = int_type:
	3’b000: u8.
	3’b001: s8.
	3’b010: u16.
	3’b011: s16.
	3’b100: u32.
	3’b101: s32.
	3'b110: s4
	3'b111: s64
Pg	Is the Predicate Register name, the predication mode is zeroing.
#sat	Is the 1-bit rounding mode:
	1’b0, no saturation
	1’b1, saturation
#rnd	Is the 3-bit round mode:
	3’b000, #r: round to nearest, tie to even(c language rint).
	3’b001, #a: round to nearest, tie away from zero(c language round).
	3’b010, #f: round to minus infinity(c language floor).
	3’b011, #c: round to positive infinity(c language ceil).
	3’b100, #z: round to zero(c language truncation).
#part	Is the 1-bit even or odd indicator:
	1’b0, #e: even part.
	1’b1, #o: odd part.
#pp	Is the 2-bit part indicator (N is a natural number):
	2’b00, #p0: index equaling to 4*N.
	2’b01, #p1: index equaling to 4*N+1.
	2’b10, #p2: index equaling to 4*N+2.
	2’b11, #p3: index equaling to 4*N+3.
sections.note: The rounding mode #ro = #o, Round to Odd rounding mode, is not defined by the IEEE 754-2008 standard. This rounding mode ensures that if the result of the conversion is inexact the least significant bit of the mantissa is forced to 1. This rounding mode enables a floating-point value to be converted to a lower precision format via an intermediate precision format while avoiding double rounding errors. For example, a 32-bit floating-point value can be converted to a correctly rounded s8 value by first using this instruction to produce a 16-bit value and then using another instruction with the wanted rounding mode to convert the 16-bit value to the final s8 value.

sections.function_description:
Conversion from integer to floating-point.


For conversion from wider data type to narrower data type, the destination element is placed into the even part or odd part of each wider element position while the other part filled with all zeros, which implies a zeroing predication mode even if this instruction have no Pg for predication.
 
Figure 13 1 wider to narrower

For conversion from narrower data type to wider data type, only even or odd input elements are converted, the other part is unused and neglected.
 
Figure 13 2 narrower to wider
For saturation conversation, any converting result exceeding the maximum and minimum range will be saturated.
For VCVTIF.s42f16, which is the reverse of VCVTFI.f162s4, 2 s4 source elements are continuously stored in Vn, i.e.
 
Predication Pg is always applied to the input lanes, for wider, narrower or same-width conversion. 
For wider/narrower conversion, predication must be merged with #part/#pp indicator to determine which input/output lane is valid. Take conversion between 32bit and 16bit data type as example:
 
Figure 13 3 narrower to wider

  Figure 13 4 wider to narrower
sections.parameter_description:
Vd	Is the destination Vector Register name.
Vn	Is the source Vector Register name.
type	Is the 5-bit convert type indicator
type={int_src_type, fp_dst_type}
type[4:2] = int_type:
	3’b000: u8.
	3’b001: s8.
	3’b010: u16.
	3’b011: s16.
	3’b100: u32.
	3’b101: s32.
	3'b110: s4
	3'b111: s64
type[1:0] = fp_type:
	2’b00:f32
	2’b01:f16
	2’b10:bf16
Pg	Is the Predicate Register name, the predication mode is zeroing.
#sat	Is the 1-bit rounding mode:
	1’b0, no saturation
	1’b1, saturation
#rnd	Is the 3-bit round mode:
	3’b000, #r: round to nearest, tie to even(c language rint).
	3’b001, #a: round to nearest, tie away from zero(c language round).
	3’b010, #f: round to minus infinity(c language floor).
	3’b011, #c: round to positive infinity(c language ceil).
	3’b100, #z: round to zero(c language truncation).
#part	Is the 1-bit even or odd indicator:
	1’b0, #e: even part.
	1’b1, #o: odd part.
#pp	Is the 2-bit part indicator (N is a natural number):
	2’b00, #p0: index equaling to 4*N.
	2’b01, #p1: index equaling to 4*N+1.
	2’b10, #p2: index equaling to 4*N+2.
	2’b11, #p3: index equaling to 4*N+3.
sections.note: The rounding mode #ro = #o, Round to Odd rounding mode, is not defined by the IEEE 754-2008 standard. This rounding mode ensures that if the result of the conversion is inexact the least significant bit of the mantissa is forced to 1. This rounding mode enables a floating-point value to be converted to a lower precision format via an intermediate precision format while avoiding double rounding errors. For example, a 32-bit floating-point value can be converted to a correctly rounded s8 value by first using this instruction to produce a 16-bit value and then using another instruction with the wanted rounding mode to convert the 16-bit value to the final s8 value.

sections.title: Distribution Histogram

doc_references.note: Documented through the shared VPTO histogram section.

sections.function_description:
Distribution Histogram Instructions.
This instruction read VL_8 uint8 elements from Vn and compute the uint16 distribution histogram of VL_16 bins, which will be further accumulated to Vd.
#bin is 4bit unsigned immediate to select bin range. Valid #bin varies with VL. As there are totally 256 bins for uint8 inputs, #bin should be less than 256/VL_16. 
For example, 
if VL=256B, #bin[0]=0/1 represents bin0~127 and bin128~255 respectively. And #bin[3:1] is neglected
if VL=128B, #bin[1:0]=0/1/2/3 represents bin0~63, bin64~127, bin128~191, bin192~255. And #bin[3:2] is neglected.
Pg is only utilized to select active input elements from Vn and won’t influence writing behavior to Vd, i.e. all the accumulation results will be written to Vd.
sections.parameter_description:
Vd	Is the destination Vector Register name.
Vn	Is the source Vector Register name.
Pg	Is the source Predicate Register name.

sections.title: VBS32.type [Xd],[Xn],[Xm],Xt

doc_references.note: This ISA mnemonic has a standalone VPTO bitonic sort section.

vpto_references.note: VPTO documents the mnemonic through a family-level op rather than a distinct VISA encoding.

sections.function_description:
type={f16, f32}   
Vector Bitonic Sorter
Sort 32 input region prososals according to the "score" element, output is 32 sorted region proposals.
The proposal with highest score is stored in the lowest address. The proposal with lowest score is stored in the highest address.
When input_proposal[i].score == input_proposal[j].score(i > j),  then input_proposal[j] will be chosen first.

First of all, INDEXes and SCOREes are separately stored in Unified Buffer
For FP32, index and score are 4Byte
For FP16, index is 4Byte and score is 2Byte.
VBS32 will combine index with corresponding score after sorting, which is a structure that occupies 8Byte with index in the upper 4 Bytes and score in the lower 4Byte. If .type=.f16, then the score is in the lower 2 byte of the 4-byte score part with the upper 2 byte reserved.
 
Xd: start address of destination structure in Unified Buffer, 32B aligned
Xn: start address of score in Unified Buffer, 32B aligned. Data are continuously stored in Unified Buffer.
Xm: start address of index in Unified Buffer, 32B aligned both source and destination region proposals are continuously stored in the memory

Xt[63:56] : repeat times, each repeat, source address and destination address will point to the next adjacent 32 indexes and scores(address plus 64B(f16)/128B(f32) for [Xn], 128B for [Xm], 256B for [Xd]), respectively. Maximum value of repeat times is 255. Xt[63:56]=1 means this instruction just runs once. Xt[63:56]=2 means this instruction runs twice.
sections.demonstration:
Notes
	repeat time = 0 means no execution, this instruction will be regarded as NOP and an warning will be reported.

sections.function_description:
Vector Merge Sorter that able to output 2 proposals per cycle.
type={f16, f32}.
Input are 4 proposal lists, each list contain sorted region proposals, output is only one sorted list, the number of elements in the output list is the sum of all 4 input lists size.
Xd: start address of destination region proposals in unit of Byte and should be 32B aligned

Xn: Xn[15:0], Xn[31:16], Xn[47:32], Xn[63:48] indicate the start addresses of four lists in unified buffer respectively. These four start addresses can be discrete in the unified buffer. And region proposals in each list must be continuous in Unified Buffer. Since each proposal structure is 8B after VBS32, each start address is in uint of 8B for f16 and f32. 
The sorter module should access UB by 32B address, then pick the proper data.

Xn[15:0] is list 1, 
Xn[31:16] is list 2, 
Xn[47:32] is list 3, 
Xn[63:48] is list 4. 
For example, Xn[15:0]=3, which means 24B for f16 and f32. 

Source address space can't be overlapped with destination address space.
When input_list[i].score == input_list[j].score(i > j), then input_list[j] will be chosen first.

Xm:Xm[15:0], Xm[31:16], Xm[47:32], Xm[63:48] indicate the element number of each lists in unified buffer respectively. 
Xm[15:0]:  number ofstructures in input list 0
Xm[31:16]: number of structures in input list 1
Xm[47:32]: number of structures in input list 2
Xm[63:48]: number of structures in input list 3

Xt[7:0]: repeat time. 
When Xt[7:0] > 1, repeat mode is enabled, and all the 4 conditions listed below must be guaranteed.
1) All input lists is continous in unified buffer. And output should stay in unified buffer as well. 
2) Xt[12]=0
3) Xt[11:8]==4'b1111
4) Software should guarantee that all 4 list number in UB must be equal.

When repeat mode is enabled, at each iteration of repeat:
Source address of list[i](0<=i<4) in repeat[j](j>=0) in UB is computed as
(Xn[15:0] + Xm[15:0]*i + Xm[15:0]*4*j)*8Byte
Destination address in repeat[j] (j>=0) in UB is computed as
Xd = Xd + Xm[15:0]*32
Xn[63:16] and Xm[63:16] are neglected when repeat mode is enabled.

Xt[12]: 
1-enable input list exhausted suspension, this is used when not all input region proposals stored in unified buffer. When one of the input lists is exhausted then this instruction stops, and writes the current executed number of regions of each list into VMS4_SR. VMS4_SR register definition is shown as below.
VMS4_SR: Vector merge sorter instruction status register, this register records the already processed region proposals in four lists
VMS4_SR [15:0]: number of finished region proposals in list0
VMS4_SR [31:16]: number of finished region proposals in list1
VMS4_SR [47:32]: number of finished region proposals in list2
VMS4_SR [63:48]: number of finished region proposals in list3
Note that under exhausted mode, when there is only one proposal left in certain list and this one is the biggest among all the enable lists, only this proposal will be generated to the output and the execution of VMS4v2 stops, with VMS4_SR of corresponding list incremented by 1 and VMS4_SR of other lists maintain its original value.
0-disable input list exhausted suspension, this is used when input data and output result can be all stored in unified buffer. When the instruction is done, it dose not update the VMS4_SR.
Xt[11:8]: 4-bit mask signal ,to indicate which of the 4 input list is valid. The valid configurations can be any 4bit digits except 4’b0000.

Notes
	Source address space can’t be overlapped with destination address space.
	The unit of src and dst address is different. 
	repeat time = 0 means no execution, this instruction will be regarded as NOP and an warning will be reported.

Demonstrations
This chapter will introduce some programming demonstrations on the new features of this ISA and will give some examples on several usual application cases.

Compiler Optimization Opportunity
To optimize execution performance in Execution Stage, several compiler techniques could be used.
Exceptions
UB Aceess Address Overflow
Load Instructions
For Load instructions in Chapter 7, if UB read address exceeds UB maximum size, exception will be raised.
Especially for Charlotte, scenarios includes AIV UB read address exceeds AIC_UB_BASE_OFFSET and AIC UB read address exceeds UB maximum size. 
Store Instructions
For store instructions in Chapter 8, if UB write address exceeds UB maximum size, exception will be raised.
Especially for Charlotte, scenarios includes AIV UB write address exceeds AIC_UB_BASE_OFFSET and AIC UB write address exceeds UB maximum size. 
SFU Instructions
For memory to memory SFU instructions including FIFR1/FMAX/FMIN/VBS16/VMS4/RPN_COR_DIAG2/MOV_UB_TO_UB, if UB read or write address exceeds UB maximum size, exceptions will be raised.
Especially for Charlotte, scenarios includes AIV UB write/read address exceeds AIC_UB_BASE_OFFSET and AIC UB write/read address exceeds UB maximum size. 
TSU instructions 
For all UB access TSU instructions, if UB read or write address exceeds UB maximum size, exceptions will be raised.
Especially for Charlotte, scenarios includes AIV UB write/read address exceeds AIC_UB_BASE_OFFSET and AIC UB write/read address exceeds UB maximum size. 
UB Access Alignment
Load Instructions
Load instructions should content address alignment rules described in Chapter 7, otherwise exceptions will be raised.
Store Instructions
Store instructions should content address alignment rules described in Chapter 8, otherwise exceptions will be raised
SFU instructions 
Memory-to-memory instructions should content address alignment rules described below, otherwise exceptions will be raised
	Source addresses should all be 32B aligned for FIFR1/FMAX/FMIN/VBS.f16/RPN_COR_DIAG2/MOV_UB_TO_UB
	Destination addresses should all be 32b aligned for FIFR1/FMAX/FMIN/VBS.f16/RPN_COR_DIAG2/MOV_UB_TO_UB
	Destination addresses should all be 32b aligned for VMS4.f16
Invalid Operands for Computation
INF/NAN
If INF/NAN exists in source operands in Instructions in Chapter 11, Chapter 12 and VBS/VMS4, exceptions will be raised.
Divided by Zero
If +0/-0 exists in Vn of VDIV/VREC/VRSQRT, exceptions will be raised.
Negative Input
If -0/Negative number exists in VLN/VSQRT/VRSQRT, exceptions will be raised.
If -0/Negative number exists in VCONV.f162u8/s162u8/s322u16/s322u8, exceptions will be raised.
